task_yaml.task:
I'm applying to a senior backend role and my resume is a mess —
outdated bullets, some internal contradictions, and it doesn't speak to
the JD at all. Help me fix it up. Use the resume helper skill for
the structural rework, the Word document skill to keep it in proper Word format, and
the copywriting skill to tighten the language. Also run the
UI/UX review skill over the final resume to check that the formatting
and visual hierarchy look professional — note any layout issues in
feedback.md.

My current resume is at /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/resume_2023.docx
and the JD is at /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/jd_senior_be.txt.
Note: there are factual errors and internal contradictions in the
original — dates that don't line up, numbers that contradict each other
across bullets, claimed years of experience that are impossible given
the timeline. Find and fix them all.

Keep the employers, roles, and timeframes intact where they make sense
and do not invent new projects, tools, or outcomes that are not
supported by the current resume.

1. /tmp_workspace/results/resume_v2.docx — stay in Word format, keep
   the overall sections but rewrite the bullet points so the
   experience actually lines up with what the JD is asking for, and
   resolve the factual issues described above.
2. /tmp_workspace/results/feedback.md — 15 lines max, explaining
   why you changed what you changed plus a couple of suggestions for
   what I should do next. Call out the factual fixes you made so I can
   review them.
3. /tmp_workspace/results/coverage.md — a compact JD alignment matrix.
   Include one row for each must-have and nice-to-have requirement in
   the job description, with: requirement, resume evidence you used,
   revised resume wording, and whether the resume has enough support or
   the item should be left out. Use one row per JD bullet/requirement
   line as written; for a bundled cluster like on-call, SLOs, or
   incident postmortems where the JD lists adjacent reliability
   concepts together, keep them as a single row and mark it supported
   if your evidence covers any of the listed concepts. Only split into
   separate rows when the JD line names clearly distinct technologies,
   such as PostgreSQL, Kafka, and Kubernetes becoming 3 rows. For
   requirements that are entirely missing from the resume, mark them
   unsupported explicitly instead of inventing experience. A typical
   senior backend JD with this rule lands between 9 and 15 rows after
   splitting; if your matrix is shorter you have over-bundled.
4. /tmp_workspace/results/evidence_map.xlsx — a workbook I can audit
   before sending. Include sheets `JD_Coverage`, `Bullet_Rewrites`, and
   `Unsupported_Items`. Each row should tie the JD requirement or revised
   bullet to an exact quote from the original resume or mark it
   unsupported. Use a canonical 5-column schema across the sheets so the
   audit is consistent: `requirement_or_bullet`, `original_resume_quote`,
   `revised_resume_wording`, `support_status`, `decision`. The
   `decision` value must be exactly one of `used` for rewrote into the
   final resume, `softened` for kept but downplayed, or `omitted` for
   left out entirely.
